Lecture 14: Cayley–Hamilton Theorem ​

Topics: §4.4–4.5 — Annihilating polynomials, computing An, characteristic polynomial det(λI−A), polynomial division (scalar and matrix), remainder theorem, adjugate proof of Cayley–Hamilton Date: May 4 – May 7, 2026

Overview ​

Lecture 13 gave us the adjugate identity A∗A=(detA)I and the inverse formula A−1=1detAA∗. This lecture asks a different question: can we compute A100 without multiplying A by itself 99 times?

The answer is the Cayley–Hamilton Theorem: the characteristic polynomial det(λI−A) annihilates A. For a 2×2 matrix, this reduces every power An to just αA+βI — two numbers instead of 99 multiplications.

The proof combines two ideas from earlier lectures:

  • The adjugate identity (Lecture 13): (λI−A)∗⋅(λI−A)=det(λI−A)⋅I
  • Polynomial division with matrix coefficients (new in this lecture)

1. How Do You Compute A100? ​

1.1 The Problem ​

Let A=(1234).

To compute A100 directly, we multiply A by itself 100 times. That is 99 matrix multiplications. Very painful.

Is there a shortcut?

1.2 What If We Had a Shortcut for A2? ​

Suppose someone told us:

A2=5A+2I

Then we can reduce every power of A:

A3=A⋅A2=A(5A+2I)=5A2⏟5A+2I+2A=5(5A+2I)+2A=27A+10IA4=A⋅A3=A(27A+10I)=27A2⏟5A+2I+10A=27(5A+2I)+10A=145A+54I

Every power of A is just αA+βI — two numbers!

An=αnA+βnI

where αn and βn satisfy a simple recurrence. Want A100? Just find α100 and β100.

1.3 Why Does This Work? ​

The relation A2=5A+2I means:

A2−5A−2I=0

So the polynomial f(t)=t2−5t−2 satisfies f(A)=0.

It kills the matrix A.

Proposition

Definition (Annihilating Polynomial)

A polynomial f(t)=c0+c1t+c2t2+⋯+cntn is called an annihilating polynomial of a matrix A if

f(A)=c0I+c1A+c2A2+⋯+cnAn=0.

Remark

Why annihilating polynomials matter

If f annihilates A and has degree d, then every An can be written as a combination of I,A,…,Ad−1.

The lower the degree, the shorter the formula for An.

2. Does Every Matrix Have an Annihilating Polynomial? ​

2.1 The 2×2 Case ​

Consider a 2×2 matrix A. The space R2×2 of all 2×2 matrices has dimension 4.

Now look at these five matrices:

I,A,A2,A3,A4

Five vectors in a 4-dimensional space ⟹ linearly dependent.

So there exist coefficients c0,c1,c2,c3,c4 (not all zero) with

c0I+c1A+c2A2+c3A3+c4A4=0.

This is an annihilating polynomial of degree ≤4.

2.2 The General Case ​

For an n×n matrix A:

  • Rn×n has dimension n2.
  • The n2+1 matrices I,A,A2,…,An2 must be linearly dependent.
  • So an annihilating polynomial of degree ≤n2 always exists.
matrix sizedimension of Rn×nguaranteed degree
2×24≤4
3×39≤9
10×10100≤100

Problem: n2 is big. And this argument does not tell us how to find the polynomial.

Can we find an annihilating polynomial of degree n (not n2), systematically?

3. The Characteristic Polynomial ​

3.1 Definition ​

Proposition

Definition (Characteristic Polynomial)

For an n×n matrix A, the expression

det(λI−A)

is a polynomial in λ of degree n. It is called the characteristic polynomial of A.

Why degree n? Each entry of λI−A is at most degree 1 in λ. The determinant multiplies n entries (one per column), so the result has degree ≤n. The leading term comes from the diagonal:

(λ−a11)(λ−a22)⋯(λ−ann)=λn+⋯

3.2 Example: 2×2 Matrix ​

Let A=(1234).

det(λI−A)=det(λ−1−2−3λ−4)=(λ−1)(λ−4)−(−2)(−3)=λ2−5λ+4−6=λ2−5λ−2

This is a polynomial of degree 2 in λ.

3.3 The 2×2 Formula ​

For a general 2×2 matrix A=(abcd):

det(λI−A)=λ2−(a+d)⏟tr(A)λ+(ad−bc)⏟det(A)

The coefficients are the trace and the determinant — two quantities we already know.

4. The Cayley–Hamilton Theorem ​

4.1 Statement ​

Proposition

Theorem (Cayley–Hamilton)

For any n×n matrix A, the characteristic polynomial det(λI−A) is an annihilating polynomial of A.

That is: if det(λI−A)=λn+cn−1λn−1+⋯+c0,

then An+cn−1An−1+⋯+c0I=0.

This gives us an annihilating polynomial of degree exactly n, computed systematically from det(λI−A).

matrix sizedimension argumentCayley–Hamilton
2×2degree ≤4degree ≤2
3×3degree ≤9degree ≤3
10×10degree ≤100degree ≤10

4.2 Verification ​

Let's check it for A=(1234) with det(λI−A)=λ2−5λ−2.

We need to verify: A2−5A−2I=0.

A2=(1234)(1234)=(7101522)A2−5A−2I=(7101522)−(5101520)−(2002)=(0000)✓

It works! But why does it always work? We need a proof.

5. Polynomial Division ​

5.1 Proof Idea from the Adjugate ​

Recall the adjugate identity: for any square matrix M,

M∗⋅M=det(M)⋅I.

Apply this to M=λI−A:

(λI−A)∗⏟adjugate⋅(λI−A)=det(λI−A)⋅I

Rearrange:

det(λI−A)⋅IλI−A=(λI−A)∗

The right side is a polynomial in λ (each entry of the adjugate is a cofactor — a determinant of a submatrix of λI−A, hence a polynomial in λ).

So det(λI−A)⋅I is divisible by (λI−A), with zero remainder!

The remainder of dividing by (λ−A) equals the value at λ=A. Since the remainder is zero: f(A)=0.

To make this rigorous, we need to understand polynomial division — first for scalars, then for matrices.

5.2 Dividing Numbers: A Warm-Up ​

When the numerator is bigger than the denominator, extract the integer part:

72=3⏟quotient+1⏞remainder2135=2⏟quotient+3⏞remainder5

The pattern: big numbersmall number=integer+remaindersmall number

Can we do the same thing for polynomials?

5.3 Dividing Polynomials ​

Consider the fraction t3+2t−1.

The numerator t3+2 has degree 3, the denominator t−1 has degree 1. Since degree of numerator > degree of denominator, we can extract a polynomial quotient:

t3+2t−1=(⋯)⏟quotient (degree 2)+remaindert−1

Long division gives us the quotient and remainder:

t2+t+1t−1t3+t2+t+2t3−t2―t2+t+2t2−t―t+2t−1―3

Quotient: t2+t+1. Remainder: 3.

t3+2t−1=t2+t+1+3t−1

Multiply both sides by (t−1):

t3+2=(t−1)(t2+t+1)+3

This is the division formula:

f(t)=(t−a)⏟divisor⋅Q(t)⏟quotient+R⏟remainder

6. The Remainder Theorem ​

6.1 The Remainder Equals f(a) ​

Look at the division formula again:

t3+2=(t−1)(t2+t+1)+3

Plug in t=1:

13+2⏟=3=(1−1)⏟=0⋅(⋯)+33=0+3✓

The remainder 3 equals the value of f(t)=t3+2 at t=1. Is this a coincidence?

6.2 Why It Always Works ​

Dividing f(t) by (t−a) gives:

f(t)=(t−a)⋅Q(t)+R

where R is a constant (degree of remainder < degree of divisor). Now plug in t=a:

f(a)=(a−a)⏟=0⋅Q(a)+R=R

So the remainder is always equal to f(a).

Proposition

Theorem (Remainder Theorem)

Let f(t) be any polynomial and let a be any number. Then

f(t)=(t−a)⋅Q(t)+f(a)

for some polynomial Q(t). In words: the remainder of dividing f(t) by (t−a) equals f(a).

Two ways to find the remainder:

  1. Long division — divide step by step, read off the remainder.
  2. Direct substitution — just compute f(a). That is the remainder.

6.3 Examples ​

Example

Example 6.1: (t2−5t−2)÷(t−1)

Step 1. Predict the remainder by direct substitution:

f(1)=12−5(1)−2=1−5−2=−6

Step 2. Verify by long division:

t2−5t−2t−1=t−4+−6t−1✓

Example

Example 6.2: (t2−5t−2)÷(t−3)

Step 1. Predict: f(3)=9−15−2=−8

Step 2. Verify:

t2−5t−2t−3=t−2+−8t−3✓

6.4 When Is the Remainder Zero? ​

From f(t)=(t−a)⋅Q(t)+f(a):

If f(a)=0, the remainder vanishes and (t−a) divides f(t) exactly:

f(t)=(t−a)⋅Q(t)

Proposition

Corollary (Factor Theorem)

(t−a) divides f(t) if and only if a is a root of f, i.e., f(a)=0.

Example

Example 6.3: f(t)=t2−5t+6. Then f(2)=4−10+6=0,

so (t−2) divides f(t). Indeed t2−5t+6=(t−2)(t−3).

7. Dividing by (λ−A): Matrix Polynomial Division ​

7.1 Back to Cayley–Hamilton ​

Recall our proof strategy:

  1. The adjugate identity tells us det(λI−A)⋅I=(λI−A)∗⋅(λI−A), so det(λI−A)⋅I is divisible by (λI−A).

  2. The remainder of dividing by (λ−A) equals the value at λ=A.

  3. Since the division is exact (zero remainder), plugging in λ=A gives 0.

To make this rigorous, we need polynomial division with matrix coefficients.

7.2 A Tempting but Wrong Proof ​

Attention

The False Proof

"Proof" of Cayley–Hamilton:

det(λI−A)|λ=A=det(AI−A)=det(0)=0.WRONG!

The error: you cannot plug a matrix into det(λI−A) directly.

The λ inside det(λI−A) sits in a scalar slot. Replacing it with a matrix changes the meaning of the expression entirely.

Consider det(λI2)=λ2. The scalar polynomial is f(λ)=λ2.

Plug in B=(1234):

  • Correct: expand first, then substitute. f(B)=B2=(7101522) (a 2×2 matrix)
  • Wrong: substitute inside the determinant. det(B⋅I2)=det(B)=−2 (a number!)
(7101522)≠−2They are not even the same type of object.

The correct approach:

Attention

The Correct Procedure

Step 1. Expand det(λI−A) to get a scalar polynomial:

det(λI−A)=λ2−5λ−2

Step 2. Substitute λ=A into the expanded polynomial:

f(A)=A2−5A−2I

Expand first, then substitute. Never substitute a matrix directly into a determinant.

7.3 Long Division with Matrix Coefficients ​

We treat A as a constant and divide:

λ2−5λ−2λ−A

The divisor (λ−A) has leading coefficient 1 (it is monic), so long division works exactly as before. The only difference: quotient and remainder now have matrix coefficients.

λ+(A−5I)λ−Aλ2−5λ+2−2Iλ2−Aλ―(A−5I)λ−2I(A−5I)λ−(A2−5A)―A2−5A−2I

Quotient: λI+(A−5I). Remainder: A2−5A−2I.

λ2−5λ−2λ−A=λI+(A−5I)+A2−5A−2Iλ−A

Multiply both sides by (λ−A):

(λ2−5λ−2)⋅I=[λI+(A−5I)]⋅(λI−A)+A2−5A−2I

The remainder A2−5A−2I is exactly f(A) — just like the scalar case!

7.4 Matrix Remainder Theorem ​

Why does the remainder equal f(A)? The same argument as before. We have the division formula:

f(λ)⋅I=Q(λ)⋅(λI−A)+R

where R is a constant matrix (does not depend on λ). Plug in λ=A into the expanded polynomial:

f(A)=Q(A)⋅(AI−A)⏟=0+R=R

So R=f(A), exactly as in the scalar case.

Proposition

Theorem (Matrix Remainder Theorem)

f(λ)⋅I=Q(λ)⋅(λI−A)+f(A)

The remainder of dividing f(λ)⋅I by (λI−A) equals f(A).

Proposition

Existence and Uniqueness of Matrix Polynomial Division

For any monic divisor d(λ) (leading coefficient =I), the division

F(λ)=Q(λ)⋅d(λ)+R(λ),deg⁡R<deg⁡d

always exists and is unique.

  • Existence: the long division algorithm works because the leading coefficient I is invertible — each step reduces the degree by 1.
  • Uniqueness: if two decompositions exist, their difference forces a degree contradiction.

8. Proof of the Cayley–Hamilton Theorem ​

Now we have all the tools.

8.1 The Adjugate Identity ​

Recall: for any square matrix M, the adjugate identity gives M∗⋅M=det(M)⋅I.

Apply this to M=λI−A:

(λI−A)∗⋅(λI−A)=det(λI−A)⋅I

Rearrange:

det(λI−A)⋅IλI−A=(λI−A)∗

8.2 The Adjugate Is a Polynomial ​

For A=(1234), the matrix λI−A=(λ−1−2−3λ−4).

The adjugate (swap diagonal, negate off-diagonal):

(λI−A)∗=(λ−423λ−1)

Write as a polynomial in λ with matrix coefficients:

(λI−A)∗=(1001)λ+(−423−1)

Each entry of (λI−A)∗ is a cofactor = determinant of a submatrix of λI−A, hence a polynomial in λ. There is no (λI−A)−1 term — the adjugate is a pure polynomial.

Verify that (λI−A)∗⋅(λI−A)=det(λI−A)⋅I:

(λ−423λ−1)(λ−1−2−3λ−4)=((λ−4)(λ−1)−6−2(λ−4)+2(λ−4)3(λ−1)−3(λ−1)−6+(λ−1)(λ−4))=(λ2−5λ−200λ2−5λ−2)=(λ2−5λ−2)⋅I✓

8.3 Two Decompositions of the Same Fraction ​

We now have two ways to write det(λI−A)⋅IλI−A:

Way 1 — polynomial division (from §7):

f(λ)⋅IλI−A=Q(λ)+f(A)λI−A

where f(A)=A2−5A−2I is the remainder.

Way 2 — adjugate identity (from §8.1):

f(λ)⋅IλI−A=(λI−A)∗

The right side is a pure polynomial — no (λI−A)−1 term!

8.4 Comparing the Two ​

Setting them equal:

Q(λ)⏟polynomial+f(A)λI−A⏟not polynomial=(λI−A)∗⏟polynomial

Move the polynomial parts to one side:

f(A)λI−A=(λI−A)∗−Q(λ)⏟polynomial in λ

The left side has (λI−A)−1, which →0 as λ→∞.

A polynomial that →0 as λ→∞ must be identically zero.

Therefore f(A)⋅(λI−A)−1=0, so f(A)=0. ◻

8.5 The Full Theorem ​

Proposition

Theorem (Cayley–Hamilton) — Proved!

For any n×n matrix A:

if det(λI−A)=λn+cn−1λn−1+⋯+c0,

then An+cn−1An−1+⋯+c0I=0.

Proof summary:

  1. Expand det(λI−A)=f(λ), a scalar polynomial of degree n.
  2. Polynomial division: f(λ)⋅I=Q(λ)⋅(λI−A)+f(A).
  3. Adjugate identity: f(λ)⋅I=(λI−A)∗⋅(λI−A).
  4. Comparing: the adjugate decomposition has zero remainder.
  5. By uniqueness: f(A)=0. ◻

Remark

A beautiful side product: the adjugate is a polynomial in A

This proof gives us more than just Cayley–Hamilton. The zero remainder tells us that f(A)=0 — but look at what happens to the quotient.

Comparing the two decompositions:

f(λ)⋅I=Q(λ)⏟quotient⋅(λI−A)=(λI−A)∗⏟adjugate⋅(λI−A)

By uniqueness: Q(λ)=(λI−A)∗.

This means the adjugate (λI−A)∗ — which we defined using cofactors and determinants — is exactly the quotient from polynomial division of the characteristic polynomial by (λ−A). It is a polynomial in λ with matrix coefficients, with no mysterious (λI−A)−1 term.

In other words, the adjugate identity M∗M=det(M)I is not just a tool we borrowed from Lecture 13 — it is the polynomial division. The entire machinery fits together: the adjugate provides the quotient, zero provides the remainder, and Cayley–Hamilton falls out as a consequence.

This is why the proof via the adjugate is considered one of the most elegant in linear algebra: every piece has a clear interpretation, and there are no wasted parts.

9. Application: Computing Matrix Powers ​

9.1 The Method ​

For A=(1234), Cayley–Hamilton gives A2=5A+2I.

Every power reduces to An=αnA+βnI:

A3=A⋅A2=A(5A+2I)=5A2+2A=5(5A+2I)+2A=27A+10I=27(1234)+10(1001)=(375481118)A4=A⋅A3=A(27A+10I)=27A2+10A=27(5A+2I)+10A=145A+54I=(199290435634)

9.2 The Recurrence ​

The coefficients follow a pattern. If An=αnA+βnI, then:

An+1=αnA2+βnA=αn(5A+2I)+βnA=(5αn+βn)A+2αnI

So: αn+1=5αn+βn, βn+1=2αn.

Starting from A1=1⋅A+0⋅I (i.e., α1=1,β1=0):

nαnβn
110
252
32710
414554

Remark

Looking ahead

The Cayley–Hamilton Theorem reduces An to a linear combination of I,A,…,An−1 — but the coefficients αn,βn still grow via a recurrence. In Lecture 15, we will discover the spectral decomposition A=λ1P1+λ2P2, which gives a closed formula:

An=λ1nP1+λ2nP2

No recurrence needed — just plug in n. The key tool will be Lagrange interpolation, which generalizes this lecture's Remainder Theorem to multiple roots.

Summary ​

ResultSource
Annihilating polynomial exists (degree ≤n2)Dimension argument
Characteristic polynomial det(λI−A)Degree n, computable
Remainder Theorem: remainder of f(t)÷(t−a)=f(a)Plug t=a into division formula
Matrix Remainder TheoremSame argument with matrix coefficients
Adjugate identity: (λI−A)∗⋅(λI−A)=f(λ)⋅IZero remainder (from Lecture 13)
Cayley–Hamilton: f(A)=0Uniqueness of polynomial division

Exercises ​

Problem

Exercise 1: Characteristic Polynomial

Let B=(2103).

(a) Compute det(λI−B).

(b) Use the Cayley–Hamilton theorem to express B2 in terms of B and I.

(c) Compute B5.

Problem

Exercise 2: The False Proof

Explain precisely why the following "proof" of Cayley–Hamilton is wrong:

f(A)=det(AI−A)=det(0)=0.

Give a concrete 2×2 example showing that det(B⋅I2)≠f(B) in general.

Problem

Exercise 3: Remainder Theorem Practice

Find the remainder when f(t)=t3−2t2+t−5 is divided by:

(a) (t−1)
(b) (t−2)
(c) (t+1)

Problem

Exercise 4: 3×3 Cayley–Hamilton

Let C=(100020003).

(a) Compute det(λI−C).

(b) Verify the Cayley–Hamilton theorem by showing f(C)=0.

(c) Express C2 in terms of C2... wait — for a diagonal matrix, can you see directly why Cayley–Hamilton holds? (Hint: what are the roots of f?)

Problem

Exercise 5: Matrix Polynomial Division

Perform the long division (λ2−5λ+6)÷(λ−A) where A=(2003).

What is the remainder? Verify that it equals f(A)=A2−5A+6I.
